_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning that the nervous and endocrine systems work together to detect stimuli, coordinate responses and maintain a stable internal environment through feedback mechanisms.

_source.w: We are learning that the nervous and endocrine systems work together to detect stimuli, coordinate responses and maintain a stable internal environment through feedback mechanisms.
y: Understanding homeostasis — the body's ability to maintain a stable internal environment — explains how we survive in changing conditions and why disruptions to these systems cause disease.
The interplay between the fast electrical signals of the nervous system and the slower chemical signals of the endocrine system demonstrates the elegance and complexity of biological coordination.
This knowledge has direct applications in medicine, pharmacology and mental health, where many treatments work by modifying nervous or endocrine system function.
eg: Tracing the nervous system pathway of a reflex arc — from receptor to sensory neuron to relay neuron to motor neuron to effector — and explaining why reflexes are faster than conscious responses.
Modelling the feedback loop that maintains blood glucose levels, involving the pancreas, insulin, glucagon and target cells in the liver and muscles.
Investigating how the endocrine system uses hormones like adrenaline to prepare the body for 'fight or flight' — increasing heart rate, dilating pupils and redirecting blood flow.
Comparing the speed, duration and specificity of nervous system responses (fast, short-lived, targeted) with endocrine responses (slower, longer-lasting, widespread).
Analysing how thermoregulation involves both nervous (detecting temperature, triggering shivering) and endocrine (metabolic rate adjustment via thyroid hormones) mechanisms.
Discussing how disruption of feedback mechanisms — such as Type 1 diabetes (insufficient insulin production) — demonstrates what happens when homeostatic systems fail.
